item.title: Uzun Hasan
item.description: Uzun Hasan Overview Uzun Hasan (c. 1423 — 1478) was the ruler of the Aq Qoyunlu (White Sheep Turkoman) confederation.

item.title: Sofia
item.description: Sofia — صوفيه (Sofya) Coordinates: 42°41’50”N 23°19’20”E Sofia (Ottoman Turkish Sofya, صوفيه) is the Balkan city that the Ottomans made the administrative capital of the province of Rumelia.

item.title: Bursa
item.description: Bursa — بروسه Coordinates: 40°11’29”N 29°04’05”E Bursa (Brusa in Western sources), the first capital of the Ottoman state from 1326 to 1402, lies at the foot of Mount Uludag (the ancient Mysian Olympus) in northwestern Anatolia.

item.title: Genoa (Ceneviz)
item.description: Genoa — جنويز (Ceneviz) Coordinates: 44°24’27”N 8°56’03”E Genoa (Ottoman Ceneviz, جنويز) was the great maritime republic of the western Ligurian coast and Venice’s principal rival for the Levant trade.

item.title: Edirne (Adrianople)
item.description: Edirne — ادرنه (Adrianople) Coordinates: 41°40’37”N 26°33’20”E Edirne, called Adrianople by Europeans, served as the second capital of the Ottoman Empire from the 1360s until the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, and remained a major imperial residence and administrative center long afterward.

item.title: Venice (Venedik)
item.description: Venice — ونديك (Venedik) Coordinates: 45°26’15”N 12°18’57”E Venice (Ottoman Venedik, ونديك) was the Ottoman Empire’s most formidable European rival and its most persistent commercial partner — the maritime republic whose galleys dominated the eastern Mediterranean and whose merchants penetrated ever...

item.title: Athens
item.description: Athens — آتنه (Atina) Coordinates: 37°59’02”N 23°43’40”E Athens (Ottoman Atina, آتنه), the ancient city of classical glory, came under Ottoman rule in 1456, three years after the fall of Constantinople.

item.title: Istanbul (Constantinople)
item.description: Istanbul — Konstantiniyye (قسطنطينيه) Coordinates: 41°00’49”N 28°57’18”E Istanbul, known to the Ottomans as Konstantiniyye (قسطنطينيه) and colloquially as Istanbul (استانبول), is the city that straddles two continents at the crossroads of Europe and Asia.

item.title: Belgrade
item.description: Belgrade — بلغراد (Nándorfehérvár) Coordinates: 44°47’13”N 20°27’15”E Belgrade, called Nándorfehérvár by the Hungarians and Belgrad (بلغراد) by the Ottomans, is the fortress city commanding the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers.

item.title: Ottoman Foreign Relations Under Bayezid II (1481-1512)
item.description: Ottoman Foreign Relations Under Bayezid II (1481-1512) The foreign relations of the Ottoman Empire during the reign of Sultan Bayezid II (1481-1512) were fundamentally shaped by a single overriding factor: the presence of his younger brother, Prince Cem (Jem), as a hostage and pawn in the hands of w...
